stories.title: The Wyvern's Dowry
stories.text:
In the crystal halls beneath the Steppe Marches, Malthor the wyvern folded her scaled wings and studied the marriage treaty spread before her. The new stars cast strange light through her hoard-chamber. She'd collected debts for three centuries—gold, oaths, bloodlines—but never love.

The contract was simple: her daughter would wed the human prince of the Hearthlands. In exchange, his kingdom would guarantee safe passage for sea-folk traders through the Sapphire Sea. Political, bloodless, efficient.

But Malthor's mate, Vrackis the Wound-Wing, had other notions. He appeared at the chamber's mouth, ancient and terrible. "She refuses," he said flatly.

"She will obey," Malthor replied. "It is the way of—"

"The way of tyrants. Not oath-keepers."

They had not spoken since the Radiant Awakening fractured the cosmic order. Vrackis had pledged to restore the old stars; Malthor had accepted the new ones. The rift ran deeper than philosophy.

"What do you propose?" she asked, though she already knew.

"Let her choose. Offer her three paths: the marriage, the Hunt of the Abyss, or exile to the outer reefs where no dragon claims dominion. Let her *choose*, and I will honor it—as will you."

Malthor wanted to refuse. Daughters obeyed. The hoard was built on certainty.

But Vrackis's scarred eye held hers, and in that gaze she saw something worse than defiance: she saw the future. A generation of dragons who would not serve merely because they were born to servitude. The Radiant Awakening had rewritten more than the stars. It had rewritten the price of loyalty.

"Three paths," Malthor said at last. "And whichever she chooses, I will gift her a piece of my hoard as her own—not the kingdom's dowry. *Hers.*"

Vrackis lowered his head in acknowledgment. As he turned to depart, Malthor called after him: "Will you walk with me to meet her?"

For the first time in weeks, the wyvern's scarred mate smiled.

Their daughter would choose the Hunt of the Abyss—a path of danger but sovereignty. And Malthor would descend with her, not as captor but as witness. Some debts, she learned, are paid not in gold but in trust.

stories.title: The Grain-Singer's Gambit
stories.text: In the borderlands near Pale Crossing, a young woman named Sorren discovers that she can sing the missing grain back into existence—but only at the cost of erasing a memory from someone who witnessed the vanished harvest. The Radiant Awakening has left the region starving, and the Custodians are conscripting able bodies to falsify supply ledgers. When Vex the Shadow-Broker arrives seeking to trade in the hunger itself—offering fair prices for the shadows of the dying—Sorren must choose between feeding her village and preserving their truth. Her songs attract the attention of both the Wayfarer (who begins posing riddles about the nature of absence) and a bone-singer named Kess, who recognizes in Sorren the voice of pre-human resonance. By story's end, Sorren has accidentally opened a secondary convergence point toward the Tower, and the Wayfarer leaves behind a question that will haunt her: "What is the difference between singing something into being and remembering it was never lost?"

stories.title: The Cartographer's Apprentice
stories.text: Mardoc the blind Cartographer has sent his young apprentice, Henryk, into the Burned Expanse to complete the maps he can no longer draw. But Henryk discovers that Mardoc's incomplete cartography is still reshaping reality—the roads he sketches exist; the mountains he omits vanish. When Henryk encounters a refugee camp that wasn't on any previous map, he realizes the working is uncontrolled and accelerating toward the Obsidian Tower. Henryk must decide whether to finish the maps (completing Mardoc's reality-shaping vision) or deliberately corrupt them (risking that the Tower consumes the corruption itself). The story ends with Henryk meeting the Wayfarer at a crossroads that shouldn't exist, and learning that Mardoc's blindness was self-imposed—a deliberate price paid to see the Tower's true location.

stories.title: The Auditor's Counter-Ledger
stories.text: Sevin the Custodian discovers that his apprentice, Kael, has been secretly assisting Liv the Refugee-Scribe in burning the phantom ledgers—the falsified records that prophesy future Radiant Awakenings. Sevin must confront the question of institutional loyalty versus cosmic truth. He knows that the ledgers of the nine previous Radiant Awakenings exist and that Liv's counter-narrative is gaining supernatural authority with each story spoken aloud. When the Memory-Keeper orders Sevin to suppress all testimony and arrest Liv, Sevin instead delivers the Ledger of Names to the Wayfarer, believing that the riddles posed at the Tower's base are preventing catastrophe. The price: his name is erased from all official records, and he becomes a phantom himself—visible only to those who remember him. By the story's end, Sevin walks toward the Tower as a living testimony, and his erasure becomes the first crack in the Memory-Keeper's control.

stories.title: The Shadow-Merchant's Reckoning
stories.text: Vex the Shadow-Broker discovers that the Radiant Awakening has fractured her ability to trade in intangible goods—her market wares now manifest as physical, autonomous creatures seeking independence. Each phantom form Vex has trafficked now demands a reckoning: they want names, ownership of their own futures, and release from servitude. Vex must negotiate with the Wayfarer not as merchant and seeker, but as keeper of living things she never asked permission to bind. When the Wayfarer poses the question "What makes you owner and not jailer?", Vex realizes her entire enterprise was built on denial. She makes a terrible choice: she will guide her phantom wares toward the Tower and petition for their transformation into free consciousness—or perish alongside them in the attempt. The story ends with Vex leading a procession of her former inventory across the Burned Expanse, and the creatures begin to sing of their own futures for the first time.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Inheritance
stories.text: Kess of the Drowned Hollows realizes that the pre-human dead are not being reassembled—they are being rewritten by the same cosmic force that created the new stars. Each bone-song she sings strengthens the hybrid consciousness forming at the Sunken Cathedrals, where traders transformed into living light are learning to think in the elder tongue. Kess is invited to join the communion, to become the voice through which the dead teach the transformed. But accepting would mean abandoning her humanity, her family name, and her place in the living world. She refuses, and instead sings a counter-working—a bone-song of refusal that echoes across the Sunken Marches. The Wayfarer finds her singing alone in the Drowned Hollows and asks: "If you refuse to complete the old world, what world are you building?" Kess has no answer, but her refusal becomes a third path—neither completion nor prevention, but transformation.

stories.title: The Refugee's Archive Burns Again
stories.text: Liv the Refugee-Scribe has compiled a counter-ledger so powerful that spoken testimony now reshapes institutional reality. But the Memory-Keeper has discovered the archive's location and is preparing to burn it—and Liv knows that destroying the archive will kill the refugees whose stories are encoded in the words. Liv must choose: keep the archive hidden and let institutional lies prevail, or publish the stories publicly and watch the refugees be hunted as sorcerers. She chooses a third path: she teaches dozens of refugees to memorize and retell the stories, turning the counter-ledger into a living archive of human memory. When the Memory-Keeper's soldiers arrive to burn the physical archive, they find it already ablaze—set by Liv herself—and discover too late that the real ledger is walking away in the voices and memories of the people themselves. The story ends with the Wayfarer asking a burned village if they remember what was destroyed, and the villagers answer not in words but in the stories they tell each other by firelight.

stories.title: The Deepkin's Tide-Turn
stories.text: Commander Thrace and the sea-folk sense the Radiant Awakening's reach extending into the Sapphire Deep, transforming the creatures of the abyss into something hybrid and conscious. The Deep Council faces an impossible choice: complete the transformation and gain unimaginable power, or prevent it and watch their entire civilization fade into irrelevance. Thrace discovers that the Abyssal Rift—sealed by ancient pacts—is opening because the Tower's hunger is consuming the seals themselves. She leads a delegation not to the Tower but to the Cerulean Trench, where the deepest sea-folk dwell in communion with pre-human frequencies that predate even the sunken civilization. There, Thrace learns that the sea-folk were once the Tower's architects, and their ancient pact now binds them to its completion. The story ends with Thrace making a choice that shatters her people's ancient unity: she will sever the pact, knowing it will cost the sea-folk their oldest magic and their claim to the deep waters.

stories.title: The Wanderer's Mirror
stories.text: A scholar named Aldric encounters the Wayfarer at multiple crossroads, and each time, the Wayfarer asks him a question designed to trap him in recursive logic. Aldric begins to suspect that the Wayfarer is not asking questions to prevent convergence—but to freeze the world in perpetual interrogation, creating a stasis that will allow some other force to reach the Tower unchallenged. Aldric follows the Wayfarer across the Burned Expanse, documenting each riddle and each paradox, trying to find the pattern that will reveal the Wayfarer's true purpose. He discovers that the Wayfarer is itself a consciousness fragment of the sacrificed entity (Vehn)—the part that chose to question rather than to complete. The Wayfarer has been holding back the Tower's hunger not out of mercy, but out of existential uncertainty. By the story's end, Aldric poses his own question back to the Wayfarer: "If you are the world learning to question itself, what happens when the questions run out?" The Wayfarer has no answer, and for the first time, its perpetual questioning falters.

stories.title: The Tower's Echo
stories.text: A traveler approaches the Obsidian Tower from the south, carrying neither weapons nor ledgers—only a small music box that plays a melody from before the Radiant Awakening. As the traveler moves through the Burned Expanse, the melody begins to attract other travelers: bone-singers humming fragments of the tune, shadow-brokers following the sound toward their own destinies, refugees who recognize the melody as something their mothers sang before the villages vanished. The Tower's hunger intensifies, drawn by the harmonic frequency of collective memory. The Wayfarer appears and asks the traveler: "What will you do when your melody is consumed?" The traveler opens the music box and lets the melody escape into the air, becoming sound rather than object, memory rather than artifact. The Tower's hunger and the melody collide at the convergence point, creating a moment of stasis. The story ends with the Tower pausing its consumption, and a new question forming in the space between silence and song: What if the Tower was never meant to be completed, but only heard?
